model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Under a fixed 256-step budget, the current script only sees about 16k training examples. Doubling batch size to 128 increases coverage to about 33k examples within the same step budget, which often helps CIFAR-10 more than preserving smaller-batch update noise in this short-run regime. Switching from Adam to AdamW with a slightly higher learning rate and stronger decoupled weight decay is a compact way to keep optimization aggressive enough for the larger batch while preserving simple, low-risk code changes. This is a fast-convergence experiment rather than a capacity change, so it fits the fixed-step setting well.","secondary_modes":["indexing","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 8","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 32","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last step improved optimization and data coverage substantially, which suggests the training setup is now less of a bottleneck than the tiny model capacity. This network is still extremely small for CIFAR-10, so a modest width increase from 8 to 16 channels and a slightly larger head is a simple next move that should improve representational power without changing the training contract or adding architectural complexity.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 2","old":"DEPTH = 1","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","old":"        self.blocks = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv2d(channels, channels, 3, padding=1, bias=bias),
            nn.BatchNorm2d(channels) if USE_BATCHNORM else nn.Identity(),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            ResidualBlock(channels, dropout=DROPOUT_RATE, use_bn=USE_BATCHNORM),
        )","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimization and width increases both paid off, so the next clean extension is a small depth increase within the same stable design. Adding one more residual block should improve feature refinement and effective receptive field while preserving the same optimizer and data-coverage setup that is already working. This is a low-complexity capacity increase compared with introducing pooling or stage changes.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The budget-matched cosine schedule hurt, so the first part of this step cleanly returns to the better constant-learning-rate regime. From there, a small amount of label smoothing is a compact regularization change that often improves CIFAR-10 validation loss even without explicit data augmentation, especially once the model has become wider and deeper. This keeps the training loop simple while targeting generalization directly.","secondary_modes":["summaries","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 8e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The best result so far came before label smoothing, and the current run suggests that extra smoothing is slightly hurting this clean-label setting. Reverting to plain cross-entropy removes that penalty, while a modest learning-rate reduction from 1e-3 to 8e-4 is a targeted refinement for the now wider and deeper model under fixed-step AdamW training. This keeps the successful architecture intact and tests whether the remaining gap is mostly step-size calibration.","secondary_modes":["caching","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 80","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The beta2 reduction was a regression, so this step restores the better AdamW setting while testing a smaller follow-on capacity increase. Width scaling has been the strongest trend so far, but the jump to a simpler head failed, suggesting the next useful change is not simplification but modestly more representational power. Increasing the trunk from 16 to 20 channels and the MLP head proportionally is a controlled extension of the best-performing design without changing the overall training dynamics.","secondary_modes":["indexing","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The latest gain came from adding capacity again, which points more toward residual underfitting than overfitting in this fixed-step regime. With the wider network, the current weight decay may now be slightly too strong and can blunt the benefit of the extra capacity. Reducing it from 5e-4 to 2e-4 is a compact follow-up that preserves the best architecture and optimizer while testing a less restrictive regularization level.","secondary_modes":["layout","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.05","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Lowering weight decay moved slightly in the wrong direction, so this step restores the better global regularization level. The new experiment is a very small explicit dropout rate, which targets the widened network more locally than stronger label smoothing did and may improve validation loss without materially changing the successful optimization setup. A 0.05 rate keeps the change conservative given the short training budget.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.05","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 160","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Explicit dropout was clearly harmful here, so this step first restores the stronger no-dropout setting. The new idea is to push data coverage a bit further within the fixed 256-step budget by increasing batch size moderately from 128 to 160 rather than jumping all the way to a much larger batch. That keeps the current successful optimizer and architecture intact while testing whether seeing more unique CIFAR-10 examples per verifier run is the next available gain.","secondary_modes":["layout","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 160","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest recent gain came from increasing batch size, which is a direct signal that this verifier benefits from seeing more unique training examples within the fixed 256-step budget. This step continues in the same direction with a moderate batch increase to 192, and pairs it with a small learning-rate bump to keep optimization aggressive as gradient noise drops. It is the most direct extension of the currently best-performing trend.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 176","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 192","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last jump crossed a stability boundary: increasing both batch size and learning rate together caused a large regression. The best result so far came from the same architecture at batch 160 and learning rate 1e-3, so the next clean test is to restore the safer learning rate and try a smaller batch-size increase to 176 instead of 192. This keeps the successful data-coverage direction while backing off from the clearly over-aggressive setting.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 184","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 176","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last step produced the strongest gain in the run, which is strong evidence that this workload is still benefiting from increased data coverage under the fixed 256-step budget. Since the larger jump to 192 was too aggressive, the next clean move is a smaller interpolation upward from 176 to 184 while keeping the rest of the recipe unchanged. This directly follows the best-performing trend with minimal added complexity.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 176","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 184","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 80","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The batch-size sweep now looks peaked around 176, so this step returns to the current best data-coverage setting rather than pushing it further. The new experiment is a modestly larger MLP head, which adds classifier capacity at very low computational cost compared with widening the convolutional trunk. Since the earlier gains came from giving the model a bit more capacity, this is a cheap way to test whether the current bottleneck has shifted into the final classifier.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The latest gain came from increasing only the classifier width while holding the rest of the successful recipe fixed, which suggests the current bottleneck may still be in the head rather than the convolutional trunk. A further increase from 96 to 128 is a cheap capacity change compared with widening all convolutions, so it preserves the best batch-size and optimizer settings while testing whether a larger final MLP can extract more from the learned pooled features.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 22","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The larger classifier head regressed, so this step restores the better 96-unit head. The new test is a small trunk-width increase from 20 to 22 channels, which follows the earlier positive trend from widening the convolutional body while keeping the currently best batch-size and optimizer settings fixed. This is a modest capacity shift from the overgrown head back into the feature extractor, where prior gains have been more reliable.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 22","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 9e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The trunk-width increase was clearly a regression, so this step restores the better 20-channel body. With the current best structure otherwise intact, the remaining plausible gain is likely a small step-size refinement rather than another capacity push. Lowering AdamW from 1e-3 to 9e-4 is a conservative test for slightly better stability at the strong 176-batch setting and the 96-unit head.","secondary_modes":["layout","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 9e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 112","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 96","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The 9e-4 learning-rate reduction regressed, so this step restores the better 1e-3 setting from the current best recipe. The new experiment is a smaller head-capacity interpolation: 96 improved over 80, while 128 was too much, so 112 is a reasonable final test for whether the classifier optimum sits between those two values while keeping the strong 176 batch size and 20-channel trunk unchanged.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}
